children.name: Platysteira
children.description: The wattle-eyes — a genus of small, robust African passerines with prominent fleshy wattles above the eyes that give them their distinctive name. The wattles are often brightly coloured in males and are used in courtship displays. They are active, restless insectivores that hawk for prey in the forest understorey, often joining mixed-species flocks.

children.name: Platysteira cyanea
children.description: A small, striking forest bird with glossy blue-black upperparts, white underparts, and vivid red fleshy wattles above the eyes, widespread across West and Central Africa. It is an active insectivore, gleaning prey from foliage and often hovering briefly to pick insects from leaves. Its loud, chattering song and distinctive wing-snapping display make it conspicuous despite its small size.

children.name: Platysteira peltata
children.description: A wattle-eye with slate-grey upperparts, a rich brown throat, and blue-grey eye wattles, found in forests and woodlands from Kenya to South Africa. It forages actively in the mid-storey, often in mixed-species flocks, taking insects from foliage and bark. Its song is a simple, high-pitched series of notes.

children.name: Platysteira albifrons
children.description: A small wattle-eye with glossy black upperparts, white underparts, a white forehead, and vivid red wattles above the eyes, endemic to the forests of Angola and the Democratic Republic of the Congo. It is a rare and little-known species restricted to the understorey of primary rainforest. Its active foraging style and wing-snapping displays are typical of the genus.

children.name: Platysteira castanea
children.description: A striking wattle-eye with glossy blue-black upperparts, a rich chestnut breast and belly, and bright red eye wattles, found in lowland forests of West and Central Africa. It forages actively in the understorey and mid-storey, often in mixed-species flocks. Its wing-snapping displays and chattering calls are characteristic of rainforest wattle-eyes.

children.name: Platysteira blissetti
children.description: A small wattle-eye with glossy black upperparts, white underparts, and vivid red wattles extending onto the cheeks, found in lowland forests of West Africa from Sierra Leone to Ghana. It is an active, acrobatic forager in the forest understorey, often joining mixed-species flocks. Its distinctive red cheek wattles give it its common name.

children.name: Platysteira jamesoni
children.description: A handsome wattle-eye with glossy blue-black upperparts, a white throat and belly, and a broad chestnut breast band, found in lowland forests of Central and East Africa. It has bright red eye wattles and an active foraging style. It is often a core member of mixed-species feeding flocks in the forest understorey.

children.name: Platysteira concreta
children.description: A distinctive wattle-eye with glossy blue-black upperparts, a white throat, and a bright yellow belly, found in lowland and montane forests of West and Central Africa. It has vivid blue-green eye wattles in the male. It forages actively in the understorey and mid-storey, often hanging upside-down to glean insects from leaves.

children.name: Batis
children.description: The batises — a genus of small, strikingly patterned African flycatchers with a distinctive habit of cocking their tails and flicking their wings. They have a bold black face mask and a white or yellow eye-ring, giving them an alert, inquisitive expression. Unlike the wattle-eyes, they lack fleshy wattles and have a more slender build adapted for active flycatching.

children.name: Batis capensis
children.description: A small, handsome flycatcher with slate-grey upperparts, a black face mask, a bold white eyebrow, and a chestnut breast band, found in forests of eastern and southern Africa. It is an active, acrobatic forager that catches insects in mid-air or gleans them from foliage, constantly flicking its wings and tail. Its simple, piping song is a familiar sound in African forests.

children.name: Batis molitor
children.description: A grey-and-white batis named for the small rufous patch on the male's white throat, widespread in woodlands and savannas across eastern and southern Africa. The male has a broad black breast band and a bold white eyebrow, while the female has a chestnut breast band. It is a vocal and conspicuous bird, often seen hawking for insects from exposed perches.

children.name: Batis diops
children.description: A striking batis with slate-grey upperparts, a bold white eyebrow, a black face mask, and a chestnut breast band, restricted to the montane forests of the Rwenzori Mountains and adjacent highlands in eastern Africa. It forages actively in the canopy, sallying for insects from exposed perches. Its high-pitched calls are a characteristic sound of the misty montane forests.

children.name: Batis poensis
children.description: A small, handsome batis endemic to Bioko Island (Fernando Po) and the adjacent mainland forests of Cameroon, with grey upperparts, a black face mask, and a white throat with a chestnut breast band. It inhabits lowland and montane forests, foraging actively in the canopy for insects.

children.name: Batis occulta
children.description: A small, grey-and-white batis with a black face mask, a white eyebrow, and a black breast band in the male, found in lowland forests of West Africa from Sierra Leone to Ghana. It was only recently recognised as a distinct species from the similar Cape Batis. It forages actively in the forest canopy.

children.name: Batis minulla
children.description: A small, striking batis with grey upperparts, a black face mask, a bold white eyebrow, and a chestnut breast band, endemic to the forests of western Angola. It is a little-known species restricted to the Angolan Escarpment forests. It forages actively in the canopy, sallying for flying insects.

children.name: Batis minor
children.description: A small, grey-and-white batis with a solid black head, a white collar, and a broad black breast band, found in woodlands and savannas of East Africa. The female has a rufous breast band instead of black. It forages actively in the canopy, constantly flicking its wings and tail, and gives a sharp, repetitive call.

children.name: Batis perkeo
children.description: One of the smallest batises, a tiny grey-and-white bird with a black face mask, a white eyebrow, and a black breast band, found in arid thorn scrub and dry woodlands of East Africa. It is a hyperactive forager, constantly on the move as it gleans insects from foliage. Its high-pitched, squeaky calls are a characteristic sound of the dry bush.

children.name: Batis ituriensis
children.description: A little-known batis restricted to the Ituri Forest of the Democratic Republic of the Congo, with grey upperparts, a black face mask, and a pale breast with a narrow black band. It inhabits the understorey and mid-storey of lowland rainforest. Its ecology remains poorly studied due to the remoteness of its habitat.
